content:
10 min read
Just now

--

SQL has been around for decades, yet it’s one of the few technologies that seems to become more valuable every year. Whether you’re building backend services, analyzing business data, training machine learning models, or preparing for software engineering interviews, you’ll almost certainly spend time writing SQL queries. It’s one of those skills that quietly appear in nearly every technical role, even if it isn’t listed as the primary requirement.
When I decided to compare SQL courses, I wasn’t looking for the platform with the highest ratings or the most polished marketing. I wanted to find courses that actually prepare people for how SQL is used in production. After working through ten popular SQL courses and learning platforms, I realized there was a significant difference between courses that teach SQL syntax and courses that teach you how to think about data.
Having spent more than eight years building backend systems and working closely with databases, SQL has been part of almost every project I’ve worked on. I’ve used it to optimize slow queries, troubleshoot production issues, design schemas, review analytics dashboards, and prepare engineers for technical interviews. Those experiences shaped how I evaluated these courses because learning SQL isn’t just about remembering commands — it’s about learning how data flows through real applications.
If you’re learning SQL to become a software engineer, data analyst, data engineer, or AI engineer, this guide will help you choose the right course for your goals. Instead of simply ranking the most popular platforms, I’ll explain what each course does well, who it’s best suited for, and how I’d approach learning SQL if I were starting again today.
What Most SQL Course Rankings Get Wrong
They Focus Too Much on Syntax
One thing I noticed is that a lot of people spend most of their time comparing beginner topics. They tell you which course teaches SELECT, JOIN, GROUP BY, or subqueries, but those features exist in nearly every SQL course.
The real question isn’t whether a course covers SQL syntax. Almost all of them do. The important question is whether the course teaches you how to solve real data problems. Writing a JOIN is easy once you’ve memorized the syntax. Knowing which JOIN to use, how to avoid duplicate records, or why a query performs poorly is what separates beginners from experienced developers.
They Ignore Real-World Database Work
Another common problem is that many rankings treat SQL as if it’s only useful for data analysis. In reality, software engineers use SQL every day to debug production systems, investigate customer issues, optimize APIs, and improve application performance.
The best SQL courses don’t just teach queries. They teach you how databases work, how schemas are designed, and how SQL fits into larger software systems. Those are the skills that continue paying dividends long after you’ve completed your first course.
How I Evaluated These Courses
Every course I reviewed teaches the basics. That’s expected.
Instead, I looked for platforms that covered topics like indexing, query optimization, normalization, transactions, and practical database design. Those concepts make a much bigger difference once you start working on real applications.
SQL is one of those skills that improves through repetition. Reading about queries only gets you so far. The strongest courses encouraged learners to solve increasingly difficult problems instead of simply following along with examples.
A great SQL course should leave you prepared for much more than writing SELECT statements. It should help you understand how applications interact with databases, why performance matters, and how to reason about data in production environments.
My Top 7 SQL Course Recommendations
1. Educative — Best Overall for Software Engineers
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What It Is
Educative offers interactive SQL courses where you learn by writing queries directly in your browser. Rather than relying heavily on video lectures, the platform encourages continuous hands-on practice while gradually introducing more advanced database concepts.
Why I Recommend It
This was my favorite platform overall because it teaches SQL as part of software engineering rather than treating it as an isolated skill. After learning SQL fundamentals, you can naturally continue into database design, backend development, APIs, system design, and distributed systems.
That’s a progression I really appreciate because SQL rarely exists on its own in industry. You’re usually writing queries inside backend services, optimizing APIs, or designing databases that support real applications.
Who It’s Best For
I’d recommend Educative to software engineers, backend developers, and anyone preparing for technical interviews where SQL knowledge is expected alongside programming skills.
How I Would Learn With It
I’d begin with SQL fundamentals before moving into relational database design, indexing, transactions, and backend development. After that, I’d build a small CRUD application that interacts with a relational database. Applying SQL inside a complete application teaches lessons that isolated exercises simply can’t.
Where It Falls Short
If you’re looking primarily for long-form video instruction, the interactive format may take a little adjustment. Personally, I found it more engaging because it encouraged constant practice instead of passive watching.
2. SQLBolt — Best Free Interactive SQL Course
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What It Is
SQLBolt has been a favorite recommendation of mine for beginners because it strips away unnecessary complexity and focuses entirely on practicing SQL through interactive exercises.
Why I Recommend It
Sometimes the simplest resources are the most effective. SQLBolt introduces one concept at a time, gives you a realistic query to solve, and provides immediate feedback. That combination makes it surprisingly effective for building confidence during the first few days of learning SQL.
Who It’s Best For
Anyone completely new to SQL who wants a quick, hands-on introduction before committing to a larger course.
How I Would Learn With It
I’d complete every lesson within SQLBolt and then immediately move into a more comprehensive course covering database design and performance optimization.
Where It Falls Short
It focuses almost entirely on query writing. Topics like schema design, indexing, transactions, and production databases require additional learning.
3. Fenzo.ai — Best AI Tutor for SQL
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What It Is
Fenzo.ai takes a different approach by acting as an AI tutor rather than a traditional course. You can ask questions about queries, request alternative solutions, debug SQL statements, or generate unlimited practice exercises.
Why I Recommend It
SQL has a habit of producing confusing errors, especially when you first start working with joins, nested queries, and window functions. Having an AI tutor that explains why your query failed instead of simply showing the correct answer accelerates learning considerably.
I also found it useful for exploring different ways to solve the same problem, which is something many traditional courses don’t emphasize enough.
Who It’s Best For
Learners who like asking follow-up questions or developers preparing for SQL interviews where multiple query approaches may be discussed.
How I Would Learn With It
I’d pair Fenzo.ai with a structured SQL course and use it whenever I encountered a concept that wasn’t immediately obvious. Asking the AI to optimize queries or explain execution plans can also be a great way to deepen your understanding.
Where It Falls Short
Like any AI tutor, it works best when paired with a structured curriculum rather than replacing one entirely.
4. SQL for Data Science (Coursera) — Best for Analysts
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What It Is
The SQL for Data Science Coursera course introduces SQL through practical data analysis problems and teaches learners how to retrieve, filter, and summarize information from relational databases.
Why I Recommend It
The course does a good job connecting SQL with real business questions rather than treating queries as isolated exercises. That’s especially valuable if your goal is analytics or business intelligence rather than backend development.
Who It’s Best For
Aspiring data analysts, business analysts, and professionals working with reporting or dashboards.
How I Would Learn With It
I’d complete the coursework and then practice on public datasets using PostgreSQL or MySQL to become comfortable writing increasingly complex analytical queries.
Where It Falls Short
Software engineers will probably want additional material covering database internals and performance optimization.
5. Complete SQL Bootcamp (Udemy) — Best Video Course
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What It Is
Jose Portilla’s SQL Bootcamp remains one of the most popular SQL courses available on Udemy, covering everything from beginner queries to more advanced database operations.
Why I Recommend It
The explanations are clear, the pace is approachable, and there are plenty of exercises to reinforce each topic. It’s a solid traditional video course that balances theory with practice.
Who It’s Best For
Learners who prefer structured video instruction and want a complete SQL curriculum from beginner to intermediate level.
How I Would Learn With It
Rather than simply following the videos, I’d recreate every exercise using my own datasets and experiment with alternative query solutions.
Where It Falls Short
Like many video courses, it’s easy to fall into passive learning unless you actively practice outside the lessons.
6. Mode SQL Tutorial — Best for Real Analytics Practice
What It Is
Mode’s SQL Tutorial combines SQL lessons with realistic business datasets, allowing you to practice queries in scenarios similar to what analysts encounter at work.
Why I Recommend It
One thing I really enjoyed was the practical nature of the exercises. Instead of abstract tables filled with meaningless values, you’re solving problems that resemble genuine business questions.
Who It’s Best For
Anyone preparing for analytics interviews or working with dashboards and reporting.
How I Would Learn With It
I’d complete each lesson before attempting additional SQL interview questions using the same concepts.
Where It Falls Short
It focuses primarily on analytical SQL rather than application development.
7. Harvard CS50 SQL — Best for Understanding Database Fundamentals
What It Is
CS50 SQL teaches databases from a broader computer science perspective, helping learners understand not only how to write queries but why relational databases work the way they do.
Why I Recommend It
One thing I’ve consistently noticed among experienced engineers is that they understand the reasoning behind database design instead of simply memorizing SQL commands. This course helps develop that deeper intuition.
Who It’s Best For
Developers who enjoy understanding the theory behind databases and want a stronger long-term foundation.
How I Would Learn With It
I’d work through the assignments slowly and spend additional time experimenting with schema design, constraints, and indexing concepts.
Where It Falls Short
If you’re looking for the quickest path to writing basic SQL queries, this course may feel more academic than necessary.
Which SQL Course Should You Choose?

• If You Want to Become a Software Engineer:
I’d recommend Educative because it naturally expands into backend engineering, databases, APIs, and system design rather than stopping with SQL syntax.

• If You’re Completely New to SQL:
SQLBolt provides one of the fastest and most approachable introductions available.

• If You’re Learning for Data Analytics:
Coursera’s SQL for Data Science and Mode’s SQL Tutorial both do an excellent job connecting SQL to
